segments.inferno-12-g151: “Poi” links this action sequentially to what has just happened: “then.” “Si rivolse” is a reflexive/pronominal passato remoto, 3rd person singular, meaning “he turned himself/turned around.” “Ripassossi” is a poetic enclitic form for “si ripassò,” with the pronoun attached after the verb; here it means he crossed back again, and “’l guazzo” is the elided form of “il guazzo,” “the ford” or shallow crossing.
segments.inferno-12-g24: “Qual è...” introduces a simile: literally “as is that bull,” with the main comparison completed later by “vid’ io.” “In quella c’ha...” is an elliptical poetic phrase meaning “at that moment in which/when it has received”; “c’” stands for elided “che,” not normal modern “ci.”
segments.inferno-12-g25: The relative “che” still refers to “quel toro.” “Gir” is an archaic/poetic infinitive for “andare/gire,” and “non sa” means “does not know how,” so the clause says the bull is unable to move properly or run straight.
segments.inferno-12-g26: “Ma” contrasts the bull’s inability to go with its erratic motion. “Qua e là” means “here and there,” and “saltella” is a finite verb here, “it leaps/hops about,” though the supplied lemma is nominal.
segments.inferno-12-g27: This is the main clause completing the simile: “I saw the Minotaur do thus.” “Vid’” is elided “vidi,” first-person singular past definite; “far cotale” uses the infinitive after a verb of perception, literally “do such a thing.”
segments.inferno-12-g28: “E” coordinates the next action after Dante sees the Minotaur’s rage. “Quello accorto” refers to Virgil, “that prudent/alert one,” with “gridò” in the past definite: “cried out.”
segments.inferno-12-g29: “Corri” is a second-person singular imperative addressed to Dante. “Al varco” contracts “a il varco” and means “to the opening/passage,” the gap they must use while the Minotaur is distracted.
segments.inferno-12-g30: “Mentre che” is a fuller medieval form of “while.” “E’” is not the conjunction “and” here but a reduced pronoun meaning “he,” and “’nfuria” is elided “infuria,” “rages” or “grows furious.”
segments.inferno-12-g31: “È buon che...” is an impersonal construction, “it is good/best that,” followed by a subjunctive-like form. “Tu ti cale” means “you lower yourself / get down,” with “ti” reflexive; the form “cale” is poetic/archaic for modern “cali.”
segments.inferno-12-g32: “Così” links the action to Virgil’s command: “thus/so.” “Prendemmo via” is an idiom meaning “we took the road/way,” and “giù per lo scarco di quelle pietre” describes the route downward through the rocky debris or landslide.
segments.inferno-12-g33: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to “quelle pietre.” “Moviensi” is an archaic enclitic form, “si movevieno/si movevano,” meaning “they moved/shifted”; “per lo novo carco” gives the cause, namely Dante’s living body as a new weight in Hell.
segments.inferno-12-g34: The clause is elliptical: a finite verb such as “was” or “was going” is understood, so “Io gia pensando” means “I was already thinking.” “Pensando” is a gerund, expressing Dante’s ongoing mental action as they move on.
segments.inferno-12-g35: “E” connects Virgil’s speech to Dante’s silent thought. “Quei” is an archaic demonstrative pronoun meaning “that one,” again referring to Virgil, and “disse” is past definite.
segments.inferno-12-g36: Virgil addresses Dante directly with “Tu pensi.” “Pensare a” means “to think of/about,” so “a questa ruina” is the object of thought; “forse” softens the statement as “perhaps.”
segments.inferno-12-g37: “Ch’è guardata” is a relative passive clause: “which is guarded.” “Da quell’ ira bestial” gives the agent, the Minotaur’s savage anger, and “ch’i’ ora spensi” is another relative clause, with “i’” meaning “io” and “spensi” meaning “I extinguished/quelled.”
segments.inferno-12-g38: “Vo’” is not “I go” here but an elided form of “voglio,” “I want,” governing “che sappi,” a subjunctive: “that you know.” The second “che” introduces the content known, while “l’altra fïata ch’i’ discesi” means “the other time when I descended”; “i’” is the pronoun “io,” not the article.
segments.inferno-12-g39: This is the main content clause dependent on “che”: “this rock had not yet fallen.” “Era ... cascata” is a pluperfect construction with auxiliary “essere” plus past participle, and “ancor” means “yet/still.”
segments.inferno-12-g1: The verb “Era” is placed first and governs “lo loco” as its subject: “the place was.” “Ov’” is an elided form of “ove,” introducing the relative clause “where we came,” and “a scender la riva” expresses purpose or direction, “to descend the bank.”
segments.inferno-12-g2: “Alpestro” is a predicate adjective referring back to “lo loco,” describing it as rugged and mountainous. The conjunction “e” links this adjective to the further description that follows.
segments.inferno-12-g3: “Per quel che” means “because of that which” or “on account of what,” introducing the cause of the place’s dreadful character. “V’er’” is an elision of “vi era,” literally “there was,” and “anco” means “still/besides,” referring to something additional present there.
segments.inferno-12-g4: “Tal” is a resumptive predicate adjective: the place was “such,” preparing for the consecutive clause introduced by “ch’” in the next segment. It points forward to the result: so horrible that every sight would avoid it.
segments.inferno-12-g5: “Ch’” introduces a result clause depending on “tal”: “such that....” “Sarebbe” is conditional, giving the sense “would be,” and “schiva” is an adjective meaning averse or reluctant; “ne” refers back to the place or what was there, “of/from it.”
segments.inferno-12-g6: “Qual è” begins a comparative construction meaning “as is,” which will be answered later by “cotal,” “such.” The relative clause “che ... l’Adice percosse” describes “quella ruina,” with poetic word order: the subject is “l’Adice,” the object is the landslide or mountain-side, and “di qua da Trento” means “on this side of Trent.”
segments.inferno-12-g7: The repeated “o ... o ...” gives alternatives: “whether by earthquake or by lack of support.” Here “per” marks cause or means, and “manco” functions adjectivally with “sostegno,” meaning “lacking support.”
segments.inferno-12-g8: “Che” introduces a result clause depending on the comparison: the collapse is such “that” the rock has descended from above. “Da cima del monte” is a compressed poetic form for “from the top of the mountain,” with “del” joining “di” and “il.”
segments.inferno-12-g9: “Onde” means “from which,” referring back to “cima del monte.” “Si mosse” is reflexive or middle in form, meaning “it moved/started from there”; the listed lemma for “mosse” does not match this usage.
segments.inferno-12-g10: “Al piano” means “down to the plain,” with “a” marking direction toward a point. “È ... discoscesa” is an old/poetic perfect-like construction with “essere,” meaning the rock has fallen or come down, and “sì” means “so,” preparing for the next result clause.
segments.inferno-12-g11: “Ch’” introduces the consequence of “sì”: the rock has fallen so as to “give/provide” a path. “Darebbe” is conditional, and “a chi sù fosse” means “to whoever was above,” with “fosse” in the imperfect subjunctive after the indefinite relative “chi.”
segments.inferno-12-g12: “Cotal” completes the earlier comparison begun with “Qual è”: “such was....” The normal order would be “la scesa di quel burrato era cotal,” but Dante places “cotal” first for emphasis and metrical shape.
segments.inferno-12-g13: “E ’n su” is an elided form of “e in su,” meaning “and upon/on.” The subject is “l’infamïa di Creti,” a periphrasis for the Minotaur, and “era distesa” describes him as lying stretched out; the relative clause “che fu concetta...” explains his origin, with “fu concetta” as a passive form, “was conceived.”
segments.inferno-12-g14: “E quando” begins a temporal clause, “and when.” “Vide noi” has the object pronoun “noi” after the verb for emphasis or poetic order: “when he saw us.”
segments.inferno-12-g15: “Sé stesso” is an emphatic reflexive object, “his own self.” “Morse” is the simple past, “bit,” with the subject still being the Minotaur.
segments.inferno-12-g16: “Sì come” means “just as” or “like,” introducing a simile. “Quei cui” means “one whom,” and “l’ira dentro fiacca” has poetic word order: anger is the subject, “within” is adverbial, and “fiacca” is a verb here meaning “breaks/subdues,” though that verbal sense is not among the listed senses.
segments.inferno-12-g17: “Lo savio mio” means “my sage,” with “lo” as the masculine article before “savio.” “Inver’ lui” is an archaic/poetic form meaning “toward him,” and “gridò” is the simple past introducing Virgil’s shouted speech.
segments.inferno-12-g18: “Forse tu credi” means “perhaps you think,” and it introduces a subordinate clause with “che.” “Sia” is subjunctive after a verb of believing/thinking, and “’l duca d’Atene” is “the duke of Athens,” namely Theseus.
segments.inferno-12-g19: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to “’l duca d’Atene.” “Sù nel mondo” means “up in the world above,” i.e. in earthly life, and “ti porse la morte” has poetic order: literally “offered you death,” meaning “brought death upon you.”
segments.inferno-12-g20: “Pàrtiti” is an imperative with an attached reflexive pronoun, meaning “depart yourself” or more naturally “get away.” The listed senses treat the form as a noun and do not match Dante’s verbal use here.
segments.inferno-12-g21: “Bestia” is a direct vocative addressed to the Minotaur. It reinforces the insulting command just given.
segments.inferno-12-g22: “Ché” means “because/for,” giving the reason the beast should stand aside. “Questi” means “this one,” referring to Dante, and “non vene ammaestrato da la tua sorella” means he does not come instructed by your sister; “da” marks the agent in a passive participial construction.
segments.inferno-12-g23: “Ma” contrasts the false assumption with the true purpose: not sent by Ariadne, but going to observe punishment. “Vassi” is an archaic form of “va si,” effectively “he goes,” and “per veder” is an infinitive of purpose, “in order to see.”
segments.inferno-12-g61: “Veggendoci” is a gerund with the enclitic object pronoun “-ci,” meaning “seeing us”; it gives the circumstance that causes the following actions. “Calar” is an infinitive after the verb of perception, meaning “to descend” or “descending.”
segments.inferno-12-g62: “Ciascun” is the singular subject, “each one,” referring to the centaurs individually. “Ristette” is passato remoto of “ristare/restare,” here meaning “stopped,” a completed narrative action.
segments.inferno-12-g63: “De la schiera” means “from the troop”; Dante uses the archaic spelling “de” for modern “di.” The subject “tre” is understood before “si dipartiro,” an archaic third-person plural passato remoto meaning “three separated themselves/departed,” and “con archi e asticciuole prima elette” describes the weapons they carried, “bows and small shafts chosen beforehand.”
segments.inferno-12-g64: “L’un” is “the one,” that is, one of the three just mentioned. “Gridò” is passato remoto, “shouted,” and “da lungi” is a poetic/archaic adverbial phrase meaning “from far away.”
segments.inferno-12-g65: The question begins with “A qual martiro,” literally “to which torment,” asking what punishment or place of suffering they are coming to. “Venite voi” has inverted subject after the verb for emphasis/interrogative force, and “che scendete la costa” is a relative clause, “you who descend the slope.”
segments.inferno-12-g66: “Ditel” is “dite lo,” an imperative plural with the object pronoun attached: “tell it.” “Costinci” is an archaic place-adverb meaning “from there,” that is, from where the addressees are standing.
segments.inferno-12-g67: “Se non” is elliptical for “se non lo dite,” “if you do not”; in context it functions like “otherwise.” The listed sense for “se” does not match its actual conditional use here.
segments.inferno-12-g68: “L’arco” is the direct object, “the bow.” “Tiro” is present indicative first person singular, “I draw,” here a threat: he will draw the bow to shoot.
segments.inferno-12-g69: “Lo mio maestro” uses the older article “lo” before a possessive adjective, where modern Italian would usually say “il mio maestro.” “Disse” is passato remoto of “dire,” introducing direct speech.
segments.inferno-12-g70: “La risposta farem noi” has poetic word order: object first, then the shortened future “farem” for “faremo,” then the explicit subject “noi.” “A Chirón costà di presso” means the answer will be given to Chiron over there at close range, not to the rash speaker from afar.
segments.inferno-12-g71: “Mal fu” places “mal” first for emphasis: literally “ill/bad was.” “La voglia tua” means “your will/desire,” and “sempre sì tosta” means “always so quick,” with the moral sense of rash or hasty.
segments.inferno-12-g72: “Poi” moves the narrative forward, “then.” “Mi tentò” uses “tentare” in an older sense, “to touch” or “to tap,” with “mi” as the direct object.
segments.inferno-12-g73: “E disse” coordinates a second completed action with “mi tentò.” “Disse” again introduces Virgil’s explanatory direct speech.
segments.inferno-12-g74: “Quelli” is an archaic demonstrative pronoun used as the subject, “that one.” “È Nesso” simply identifies the figure: “is Nessus.”
segments.inferno-12-g75: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to Nessus, “who.” “Morì per la bella Deianira” means he died on account of or because of beautiful Deianira; “per” gives the cause or occasion of his death.
segments.inferno-12-g76: “Fé” is a contracted poetic form of “fece,” passato remoto of “fare,” here “made/wrought.” “Di sé la vendetta” is an idiomatic compressed phrase meaning “vengeance for himself,” and “elli stesso” is archaic for “egli stesso,” emphatically “he himself”; the provided senses for “elli” do not fit this pronoun.
segments.inferno-12-g109: “Si volse” is a reflexive past historic form, literally “turned himself,” used for a completed narrative action. “In su la destra poppa” is poetic/archaic phrasing meaning “upon/over the right breast,” with “poppa” here referring to the chest or breast, not a ship’s stern.
segments.inferno-12-g110: “E disse” continues the past-historic narration: “and he said.” The preposition “a” marks the indirect object, the person addressed, Nessus.
segments.inferno-12-g111: “Torna” is a second-person singular imperative addressed to Nessus. In context it means “turn back” or “go back,” so that Nessus can escort Dante and Virgil.
segments.inferno-12-g112: “E sì li guida” is another imperative coordinated with “Torna.” “Sì” is not an answer “yes” here but an adverb meaning “so/thus,” and “li” is an archaic object pronoun meaning “them,” referring to the travelers.
segments.inferno-12-g113: “Fa cansar” is a causative construction: “make [someone] move aside.” “S’altra” is an elision of “se altra,” meaning “if another,” and “v’intoppa” contains “vi” as an object pronoun, “comes upon you / runs into you.”
segments.inferno-12-g114: “Or” functions as a narrative transition, “then/now,” and “ci movemmo” is a reflexive first-person plural past historic, meaning “we moved ourselves,” i.e. “we set out.” “Lungo” is used prepositionally as “along,” and “del bollor vermiglio” describes the red boiling mass, the river of blood.
segments.inferno-12-g115: “Dove” introduces a relative clause locating the action at that bank. “I bolliti” is a substantivized past participle, “the boiled ones,” and “facieno” is an archaic imperfect form of “facevano,” here meaning “were uttering/making” shrieks.
segments.inferno-12-g116: “Io vidi” uses the past historic for Dante’s completed act of seeing. “Sotto infino al ciglio” means the people were under the boiling blood as far as the eyelid/brow; “infino a” is an intensified form of “fino a,” “up to/as far as.”
segments.inferno-12-g117: The phrase continues the narrative with another past-historic verb, “disse.” “’l” is the elided form of “il,” and “gran centauro” identifies Chiron as the authoritative speaker.
segments.inferno-12-g118: “E’ son” is archaic for “they are,” with “E’” as a subject pronoun. “Che” introduces a relative clause; “dier” in the text is past historic plural of “dare,” and the idiom “dare di piglio” means “to lay hands on/seize,” here applied to bloodshed and property.
segments.inferno-12-g119: “Quivi” means “there/here in that place,” referring to this part of Hell. “Si piangon” is a passive/impersonal construction, literally “one laments,” but naturally “are lamented”; “piangon” is a shortened third-person plural form.
segments.inferno-12-g120: “Quivi è” means “there is / there is located,” introducing one of the souls punished there. “Alessandro” is a named example of a tyrant among those submerged in the blood.
segments.inferno-12-g121: “Dïonisio” has a diaeresis to mark the syllables for the verse, and “fero” is an adjective meaning “fierce.” “Che fé Cicilia aver dolorosi anni” uses “fé,” a contracted past-historic form of “fece,” in a causative construction: he “made/caused Sicily to have” painful years.
segments.inferno-12-g77: This opens with the coordinating conjunction “E” and a demonstrative phrase, “quel di mezzo,” meaning “that one in the middle.” The preposition “di” marks position or relation: literally “that one of the middle.”
segments.inferno-12-g78: “ch’” is a relative pronoun referring back to “quel di mezzo.” “Al petto si mira” has reflexive “si” with “mira”: he gazes at himself, specifically at his chest; the word order is poetic but the sense is “who looks at his own breast/chest.”
segments.inferno-12-g79: This is the main predicate of the first half of the sentence: “is the great Chiron.” “Gran” is the shortened form of “grande” before a noun and here means “great” in rank or fame, not merely large.
segments.inferno-12-g80: “Il qual” is an elevated relative form, equivalent to “who” or “the one who,” referring to Chiron. “Nodrì” is a literary past tense form meaning “reared” or “brought up,” with “Achille” as its object.
segments.inferno-12-g81: “Quell’ altro” means “that other one,” picking out another centaur from the group. The verb “è” simply identifies him: “is Pholus.”
segments.inferno-12-g82: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to Pholus. “Fu sì pien d’ira” uses the past tense “fu” with “sì” meaning “so,” and “pien d’ira” is a contracted poetic form of “pieno d’ira,” “full of wrath.”
segments.inferno-12-g83: “Dintorno al fosso” gives the place of motion, “around the ditch.” “Vanno a mille a mille” uses a repeated numerical expression meaning “they go by thousands upon thousands”; the repeated “a” is idiomatic and distributive, roughly “by.”
segments.inferno-12-g84: “Saettando” is a gerund modifying “vanno”: they go around while shooting arrows. “Qual anima” means “whatever soul” or “any soul that”; “si svelle del sangue” is reflexive, “tears itself out from the blood.” The comparison “più che sua colpa sortille” means “more than its guilt allotted it,” with “sortille” an archaic poetic past form expressing what has fallen to the soul as its assigned lot.
segments.inferno-12-g85: “Noi” is explicit for emphasis, while “ci appressammo” is reflexive in form and means “we drew near” or “we approached.” “A quelle fiere isnelle” gives the object approached; “isnelle” is an archaic form meaning “nimble” or “agile.”
segments.inferno-12-g86: This clause narrates Chiron’s action with the simple past “prese,” “took.” “Uno strale” means “an arrow”; “uno” functions as the indefinite article here rather than as the number emphasized.
segments.inferno-12-g87: “E” links this action to Chiron’s taking the arrow. “Con la cocca” means “with the nock,” instrumentally; “fece la barba in dietro a le mascelle” is a compressed poetic construction meaning he made or pushed his beard back toward/behind his jaws, exposing his mouth.
segments.inferno-12-g88: “Quando” introduces a temporal subordinate clause. “S’ebbe scoperta” is an old reflexive/pronominal perfect construction with “ebbe” plus past participle; the participle “scoperta” agrees with “la gran bocca,” meaning “when he had uncovered his great mouth.”
segments.inferno-12-g89: The main verb is “disse,” simple past: “he said.” “A’ compagni” is a contracted form for “ai compagni,” meaning “to the companions.”
segments.inferno-12-g90: “Siete voi accorti” means “are you aware” or “have you noticed,” with the subject pronoun “voi” expressed for emphasis. “Che” introduces the content of their awareness; “quel di retro” is an archaic phrase meaning “the one behind.” “Move ciò ch’el tocca” means “moves that which he touches,” with “el” an archaic form of “he.”
segments.inferno-12-g122: The phrase points out a visible figure by a physical feature: "quella fronte" is literally "that forehead/brow," standing for the person whose head is visible. "c’ha" is a contraction of "che ha" ("which has"), not the pronoun "ci"; "’l pel" is "il pel," with "pel" as singular collective for hair.
segments.inferno-12-g123: "è Azzolino" gives the identification of the person just described. The verb "è" is present indicative of "essere," used as a copula: "is Azzolino."
segments.inferno-12-g124: This coordinates a second identification with the first: "e quell’altro" means "and that other one." "ch’è" contracts "che è," where "che" is a relative pronoun referring to the person, and "biondo" is a predicate adjective meaning blond or fair-haired.
segments.inferno-12-g125: This is the matching copular identification for the second figure. "da Esti" means "from Este" or "of the Este family," with "da" marking origin or family association.
segments.inferno-12-g126: "il qual" is an archaic/formal relative pronoun, "who," referring back to Opizzo. "fu spento" is a passive past-tense form, literally "was extinguished" and figuratively "was killed"; "dal figliastro" gives the agent, and "sù nel mondo" means up in the living world, as opposed to Hell.
segments.inferno-12-g127: "Allor" marks the next action, "then." "mi volsi" is the passato remoto of the reflexive/pronominal verb "volgersi," meaning "I turned," and "al poeta" gives the direction of the turning: toward the poet.
segments.inferno-12-g128: "quei" is a poetic demonstrative pronoun meaning "that one" or simply "he," here referring to the poet just mentioned. "disse" is passato remoto of "dire," the standard narrative past tense for "said."
segments.inferno-12-g129: "Questi" means "this one," referring to the centaur who is now to lead or instruct Dante. "sia" is present subjunctive used jussively: "let him be"; "ti" is a dative of reference, "for you," and "or" is the apocopated form of "ora," "now."
segments.inferno-12-g130: This clause is elliptical: the verb "sia" is understood from the previous segment, so the sense is "and let me be second." "secondo" is a predicate adjective/noun parallel to "primo."
segments.inferno-12-g131: "Poco più oltre" is an adverbial phrase meaning "a little farther on." "s’affisse" is a reflexive/pronominal past tense form meaning that the centaur stopped or fixed himself in place; the relative clause "che ’nfino a la gola parea che ... uscisse" says that the group seemed, up to the throat, to be coming out of the boiling pool, with "parea" as archaic imperfect for "pareva" and "uscisse" as imperfect subjunctive after "parea che."
segments.inferno-12-g132: "Mostrocci" is "mostrò" plus the enclitic pronoun "ci": "he showed us" or "he pointed out to us." The object is "un’ombra," and "da l’un canto sola" places and characterizes it: alone, off to one side.
segments.inferno-12-g133: "dicendo" is the gerund of "dire," expressing an action simultaneous with the pointing out. It introduces the centaur’s direct speech.
segments.inferno-12-g134: "Colui" means "that one," referring to the isolated shade. "fesse" is an archaic past form of "fendere," "cleft" or "pierced," not the noun "fere"; "lo cor che ’n su Tamisi ancor si cola" means "the heart which is still venerated on the Thames," with passive/reflexive "si cola" from the idea of revering or worshipping.
segments.inferno-12-g40: "Ma" turns from the earlier statement to a correction or qualification. "Poco pria" is an archaic/poetic form for "a little before," with "pria" meaning "before" and setting up the following temporal clause introduced by "che."
segments.inferno-12-g41: "Se ben discerno" is a parenthetical condition: "if I discern/judge well." "Discerno" is present indicative first person singular, and "ben" modifies the act of judging rather than the event itself.
segments.inferno-12-g42: "Che venisse" completes "poco pria": literally, "a little before that he should come," using the imperfect subjunctive after a temporal expression. "Colui che" means "he who," and the relative clause identifies him as the one who "levò"—removed or carried off—the "gran preda" from Dis, namely from the upper circle of Hell.
segments.inferno-12-g43: The main verb finally appears here: "l’alta valle feda tremò," "the deep foul valley quaked." "Da tutte parti" means "from/on every side," and "sì" is an intensifier anticipating the result clause introduced by "ch’" in the next segment.
segments.inferno-12-g44: "Ch’i’" contracts "che io," introducing the result of the valley’s trembling: "so that I thought." "Sentisse" is imperfect subjunctive in indirect thought after "pensai che," and "amor" refers to Love as a cosmic force felt by the universe.
segments.inferno-12-g45: "Per lo qual" means "through/by which," referring back to "amor." "È chi creda" is an impersonal expression, "there are those who believe," with "creda" in the subjunctive; "il mondo in caòsso converso" is a compressed passive idea, "the world turned into chaos."
segments.inferno-12-g46: "E in quel punto" resumes the narrative sequence: "and at that moment." "Questa vecchia roccia" is the subject of the later verb "fece," with "vecchia" meaning ancient or old, not a noun.
segments.inferno-12-g47: "Qui e altrove" is an adverbial phrase locating the same effect both in the present place and in other parts of Hell. It interrupts the subject-verb phrase before the final predicate.
segments.inferno-12-g48: "Tal fece riverso" has poetic word order: literally, "made such an overturn/collapse." "Tal" points back to the violent quake and forward to the ruined rockfall Dante is seeing.
segments.inferno-12-g49: "Ficca" is a second-person singular imperative: Virgil commands Dante to fix or fasten his eyes. "Li occhi" is an archaic form for modern "gli occhi," and "a valle" means toward/down into the valley.
segments.inferno-12-g50: "Ché" gives the reason for the command: "because/for." "S’approccia" is a pronominal verb meaning "draws near," with the subject following it, "la riviera del sangue"; "in la qual" introduces a relative clause, and "qual che ... noccia" means "whoever harms," with "noccia" in the subjunctive because the person is indefinite.
segments.inferno-12-g51: This is an apostrophe, an emotional direct address to abstract forces. "Cieca" modifies "cupidigia," and "folle" modifies "ira," presenting greed as blind and anger as mad or irrational.
segments.inferno-12-g52: "Che" links the addressed nouns to what they do, functioning like "which." "Sì" means "so," "ci" is the direct object "us," and "sproni" is a verbal form meaning "you/it spur(s)," while "ne la" is the older separated spelling of modern "nella."
segments.inferno-12-g53: "Ne l’etterna" means "in the eternal one," with "vita" understood from the previous phrase: eternal life or eternal state. "C’immolle" contracts "ci immolle," "soaks/steeps us," and "sì mal" means "so badly/miserably."
segments.inferno-12-g54: "Io vidi" is straightforward past narrative in the passato remoto: "I saw." The object is "un’ampia fossa," and "in arco torta" is a compact participial/adjectival phrase meaning "bent into an arc."
segments.inferno-12-g55: "Come quella" means "like that one," referring to a ditch or channel of the kind just mentioned. The relative clause "che tutto ’l piano abbraccia" has "che" as subject and "tutto ’l piano" as object: it surrounds the entire plain.
segments.inferno-12-g56: "Secondo ch’" means "according as" or "just as." "Avea detto" is an archaic/poetic form of "aveva detto," a pluperfect meaning "had said," and "la mia scorta" is Dante’s guide.
segments.inferno-12-g57: This prepositional phrase locates the centaurs: "between the foot/base of the bank and it." "’L" is the elided article "il," "de la" is the older separated form of "della," and "essa" refers back to the ditch.
segments.inferno-12-g58: "In traccia" means in a track, trail, or line, describing their formation. "Corrien" is a syncopated poetic imperfect form of "correvano," so the action is ongoing: "centaurs were running."
segments.inferno-12-g59: "Armati" is a past participle used adjectivally, agreeing with "centauri." "Di saette" literally means "of arrows," idiomatically "armed with arrows."
segments.inferno-12-g60: "Come solien" means "as they were accustomed," with "solien" an archaic imperfect form of "solevano." "Nel mondo" contrasts their earthly life with their present infernal role, and "andare a caccia" is the idiom "to go hunting."
segments.inferno-12-g91: The segment is a complete sentence: "Così" means "in this way/like that" and is placed first for emphasis. "Soglion" is an archaic/poetic present form meaning "are wont, usually do," followed by the infinitive "far"; "li piè d’i morti" is the subject, with archaic articles "li" and contracted "d’i" = "di i," "of the dead."
segments.inferno-12-g92: This noun phrase is the subject of the main verb "rispuose" later in the sentence. "’l" is the elided article "il," and "duca" here means not a political duke but Dante’s guide; "buon" is the shortened masculine form of "buono" before a noun.
segments.inferno-12-g93: The relative clause "che ..." describes the guide. "Er’" is elided "era," an imperfect form of "essere"; "li" here functions idiomatically as "to/at him" rather than as the article listed, so the phrase means that Virgil was already at the other’s chest.
segments.inferno-12-g94: This relative/location phrase explains what part of the centaur’s body is meant: the place "where" his two natures meet. "Son" is poetic shortened "sono," and "consorti" here means "joined, sharing together," not the senses supplied in the list.
segments.inferno-12-g95: "Rispuose" is an archaic perfect-tense form of "rispose," "answered." It is the main verb whose subject is "’l mio buon duca."
segments.inferno-12-g96: "Ben" is not merely "well" here but an emphatic adverb: "indeed, certainly." "È vivo" is a simple present predicate, stressing that Dante is physically alive.
segments.inferno-12-g97: The normal prose order would be "e mi convien mostrarli, sì soletto, la valle buia." "Convien" is an impersonal verb meaning "it is necessary," with "mi" = "to me/for me"; "li" is an archaic dative pronoun meaning "to him," not the article in the word list. "Sì soletto" intensifies "soletto," meaning "quite/all alone."
segments.inferno-12-g98: "Necessità" is the subject, almost personified: necessity is what drives or brings him here. "’l ci ’nduce" contracts "lo ci induce": "lo" = "him," "ci" is locative "here/there," and "’nduce" is an aphetic form of "induce/conduce," "brings, leads."
segments.inferno-12-g99: This elliptical phrase contrasts with the preceding subject: it is necessity, "and not pleasure," that causes the journey. "Diletto" is a noun meaning "delight" or "pleasure."
segments.inferno-12-g100: "Tal" means "such a one" or "someone," serving as the subject of both "si partì" and, through the relative "che," "commise." "Si partì da" is a reflexive/medieval construction meaning "departed from," and "cantare alleluia" evokes the blessed activity of Heaven; "commise" means "entrusted" or "assigned" this "officio novo," a new or strange duty.
segments.inferno-12-g101: This is a simple negative predicate: "he is not a robber." "Ladron" is an apocopated form of "ladrone/ladro," with the final vowel dropped for meter.
segments.inferno-12-g102: "Né" coordinates the second negative statement with the first: "nor am I ...." "Anima" is a noun here, "soul," despite the misleading lemma in the list, and "fuia" is an old adjective meaning "thieving" or "furtive."
segments.inferno-12-g103: "Ma" turns from explanation to request, and "per quella virtù" is an appeal or oath: "by that power." The relative phrase "per cu’" means "through/by which," referring to "virtù"; "movo li passi miei" is poetic for "I move my steps," and "per sì selvaggia strada" means "along/through so savage a road."
segments.inferno-12-g104: "Danne" is "da’ + ne," an imperative with enclitic pronoun, meaning "give us" in this context. "Un de’ tuoi" means "one of yours," that is, one of the centaurs or followers belonging to the addressee.
segments.inferno-12-g105: This relative clause describes the requested helper. "A cui" links back to "un de’ tuoi," while "siamo" is best read as a subjunctive-like form governed by the request; "a provo" is an archaic phrase meaning "close by, side by side."
segments.inferno-12-g106: "E che" continues the relative/purpose construction: Virgil asks for someone "who may show" them the ford. "Mostri" is present subjunctive, "ne" is an old form meaning "to us," and "dove si guada" uses impersonal "si": "where one fords" or "where it is fordable."
segments.inferno-12-g107: This is another coordinated relative/purpose clause depending on "un de’ tuoi." "Porti" is present subjunctive, "costui" points to Dante as "this man," and "in su la groppa" is a poetic prepositional expression meaning "up on/upon the back."
segments.inferno-12-g108: "Ché" gives the reason for the request: "for/because." "Non è spirto che ... vada" uses a relative clause with subjunctive "vada," expressing that Dante is not an incorporeal spirit able to move through the air.
segments.inferno-12-g135: “Poi vidi gente” gives a simple narrative sequence: “then I saw people.” The relative clause “che ... tenean” describes that people; “tenean” is poetic/archaic for “tenevano,” imperfect plural, and “di fuor del rio” means “out of/outside the river,” with the object “la testa e ... tutto ’l casso” placed after the verb.
segments.inferno-12-g136: “E” adds a further observation to what Dante saw. “Di costoro assai” is a partitive expression, “many of these people,” and “riconobb’ io” is “riconobbi io,” with the final vowel elided and the subject “io” placed after the verb for emphasis and meter.
segments.inferno-12-g137: “Così” links this description to the preceding gradations in the river. The phrase “a più a più” is an idiom meaning progressively, “more and more” or “little by little”; “si facea basso” is archaic for “si faceva basso,” literally “made itself low,” meaning “was becoming shallow.”
segments.inferno-12-g138: “Sì che” introduces the result: the blood had become so shallow that it affected only the feet. “Cocea” is an imperfect form of “cuocere,” here meaning “burned/scalded,” and “li” is an old masculine plural article for modern “i.”
segments.inferno-12-g139: “Quindi” means “from there,” referring to the shallower point just described. The verb “fu” precedes its subject “il nostro passo,” a poetic inversion: literally, “from there was our passage of the ditch,” meaning that this was where they crossed.
segments.inferno-12-g140: “Sì come” introduces a comparison, “just as.” “Da questa parte” means “on this side,” and the object of “vedi” is “lo bulicame,” the boiling pool; the relative clause “che sempre si scema” says that it continually diminishes or becomes shallower, with “si” used in a middle/reflexive sense.
segments.inferno-12-g141: This is a parenthetical speech tag inserted between parts of the centaur’s direct speech. “Disse ’l centauro” has inverted order, verb before subject; “’l” is the elided form of “il.”
segments.inferno-12-g142: “Voglio che tu credi” means “I want you to believe”; “credi” is formally indicative in modern terms but functions within Dante’s dependent construction. The second “che” introduces what Dante is to believe: “da quest’altra” means “on the other side,” and “a più a più giù prema lo fondo suo” has the subject after the verb, “its bottom sinks/presses downward lower and lower.”
segments.inferno-12-g143: “Infin ch’el” means “until it,” with “ch’” contracted before “el.” “Si raggiunge” is reflexive/middle, “rejoins itself” or “comes together again,” and “ove” introduces the place where; “convien che gema” is an impersonal necessity construction, “it is necessary that it groan,” with “gema” in the subjunctive.
segments.inferno-12-g144: “La divina giustizia” is the subject, and “punge” is present tense, here meaning “torments” or “stings.” “Di qua” means “here/on this side,” and “quell’ Attila che fu flagello in terra” is the direct object with a relative clause: Attila “who was a scourge on earth.”
segments.inferno-12-g145: “E Pirro e Sesto” adds two more direct objects governed by “punge” from the previous segment. The repeated “e ... e” coordinates the names simply: “and Pyrrhus and Sextus.”
segments.inferno-12-g146: The subject is still “la divina giustizia.” “In etterno” is an adverbial phrase meaning “forever,” and “munge le lagrime” is a vivid metaphor: justice “milks” or wrings out tears.
segments.inferno-12-g147: “Che” refers back to “le lagrime.” “Col” is the contraction of “con il,” and “diserra” means “unbars” or “releases”: the boiling heat forces the tears out.
segments.inferno-12-g148: This phrase depends on “munge le lagrime”: justice wrings tears “a Rinier,” that is, from Rinier. “Da Corneto” identifies him by origin or place-name, “from Corneto.”
segments.inferno-12-g149: This is a second person from whom tears are wrung, parallel to “a Rinier da Corneto.” “Pazzo” functions as a name or epithet, literally “mad,” attached to this Rinier.
segments.inferno-12-g150: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to the two Riniers. “Fecero ... guerra a le strade” uses “fare guerra a” in the sense “to make war on” or “to harass,” while “a le” is the uncontracted form of modern “alle.”

sentences.inferno-12-s37: Then he turned around and crossed back over the ford.
sentences.inferno-12-s5: Like that bull which breaks loose at the very moment when it has already received the mortal blow, and cannot run but leaps this way and that, so I saw the Minotaur behave; and that wary one cried out: “Run to the passage; while he is raging, it is best that you get down.”
sentences.inferno-12-s6: Thus we took our way down through the spill of those rocks, which often shifted beneath my feet because of the new burden.
sentences.inferno-12-s7: I was already thinking; and he said: “You are perhaps thinking of this ruin, which is guarded by that bestial rage which I just quenched.
sentences.inferno-12-s8: Now I want you to know that the other time when I descended down here into the lower Hell, this rock had not yet fallen.
sentences.inferno-12-s1: The place where we came to descend the bank was rugged and, because of what was there besides, such that every eye would have shunned it.
sentences.inferno-12-s2: As is that landslide which the Adige struck on its flank on this side of Trent, whether by earthquake or by failure of support, so that from the top of the mountain, whence it began to move, the rock has fallen down to the plain in such a way that it would provide some path for anyone who was above: such was the descent of that ravine; and on the edge of the broken gully lay stretched out the infamy of Crete, who was conceived in the false cow; and when he saw us, he bit himself, like one whom anger breaks inwardly.
sentences.inferno-12-s3: My sage cried out toward him: “Perhaps you think that the duke of Athens is here, who up in the world brought death upon you?”
sentences.inferno-12-s4: Get away, beast, for this man does not come instructed by your sister, but goes to see your punishments.”
sentences.inferno-12-s13: Seeing us descending, each one stopped, and from the troop three separated themselves, with bows and little shafts chosen beforehand; and one shouted from afar: “To what torment do you come, you who are descending the slope?”
sentences.inferno-12-s14: Tell it from there; if not, I draw the bow.
sentences.inferno-12-s15: My master said: “We shall make our answer to Chiron over there close by; your will was always so rash.”
sentences.inferno-12-s16: Then he touched me, and said: “That one is Nessus, who died because of the beautiful Deianira, and he himself took vengeance for himself.”
sentences.inferno-12-s25: Chiron turned on his right breast and said to Nessus: “Turn back, and guide them thus, and make them move aside if another troop comes upon you.”
sentences.inferno-12-s26: Then we set out with the trusty escort along the bank of the crimson boiling, where those who were boiled uttered loud shrieks.
sentences.inferno-12-s27: I saw people submerged beneath it up to their eyelids; and the great centaur said: “They are tyrants who laid hands on bloodshed and on possessions.”
sentences.inferno-12-s28: Here the merciless harms are lamented; here is Alexander, and fierce Dionysius, who caused Sicily to have painful years.
sentences.inferno-12-s17: And that one in the middle, who gazes at his own chest, is the great Chiron, who reared Achilles; that other one is Pholus, who was so full of wrath.
sentences.inferno-12-s18: Around the ditch they go in thousands upon thousands, shooting any soul that tears itself out of the blood more than its guilt has allotted it.
sentences.inferno-12-s19: We drew near to those nimble beasts: Chiron took an arrow, and with the nock pushed his beard back behind his jaws.
sentences.inferno-12-s20: When he had uncovered his great mouth, he said to his companions: “Have you noticed that the one behind moves whatever he touches?”
sentences.inferno-12-s29: And that brow which has such black hair is Azzolino; and that other one who is blond is Opizzo of Este, who, in truth, was put to death by his stepson up in the world.
sentences.inferno-12-s30: Then I turned to the poet, and he said: "Let this one now be first for you, and I second."
sentences.inferno-12-s31: A little farther on, the centaur stopped above a people who, up to the throat, seemed to be emerging from that boiling pool.
sentences.inferno-12-s32: He pointed out to us a shade alone on one side, saying: "That one pierced, in God’s bosom, the heart that is still venerated on the Thames."
sentences.inferno-12-s9: But certainly a little before, if I judge rightly, the coming of him who took from Dis the great spoil of the upper circle, the deep foul valley trembled on every side so violently that I thought the universe felt Love, through which some believe the world has more than once been turned into chaos; and at that moment this ancient rock, here and elsewhere, made such a collapse.
sentences.inferno-12-s10: But fix your eyes down into the valley, for the river of blood is drawing near, in which boils whoever harms another by violence.
sentences.inferno-12-s11: Oh blind greed and mad anger, which so spur us on in our short life, and then in the eternal one soak us so miserably!
sentences.inferno-12-s12: I saw a wide ditch bent in an arc, like one that embraces the whole plain, just as my escort had said; and between the foot of the bank and it, centaurs were running in a line, armed with arrows, as they used to go hunting in the world.
sentences.inferno-12-s21: The feet of the dead are not wont to act like that.
sentences.inferno-12-s22: And my good guide, who by then was at his chest, where the two natures are joined, answered: “Indeed, he is alive, and I must, all alone, show him the dark valley; necessity brings him here, not pleasure.”
sentences.inferno-12-s23: One who departed from singing alleluia entrusted this strange new office to me: he is no robber, nor am I a thieving soul.
sentences.inferno-12-s24: But by that power through which I move my steps along so savage a road, give us one of your band beside whom we may go, and who may show us the place where it is fordable, and who may carry this man on his back, for he is no spirit that can travel through the air.
sentences.inferno-12-s33: Then I saw people who held their heads, and still their whole torsos, out of the river; and I recognized many of these.
sentences.inferno-12-s34: Thus, little by little, that blood grew shallower, so that it scalded only the feet; and from there came our crossing of the ditch.
sentences.inferno-12-s35: “Just as you see, on this side, the boiling pool that continually grows shallower,” said the centaur, “I want you to believe that on the other side its bottom sinks lower and lower, until it rejoins the place where tyranny must groan.”
sentences.inferno-12-s36: Divine justice here torments that Attila who was a scourge on earth, and Pyrrhus and Sextus; and forever it wrings the tears, which it releases with the boiling heat, from Rinier da Corneto and Rinier Pazzo, who made such war upon the roads.”
